name: ANTLR4
summary: Parser generator built on Terence Parr and Sam Harwell's ALL(*) algorithm: at each ambiguous decision, parse-time subset-construction-style lookahead replaces compile-time grammar analysis. Linear on real grammars; O(n⁴) worst case.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Terence Parr and Sam Harwell's ALL(*) algorithm (OOPSLA 2014, with Kathleen Fisher) is the engine behind ANTLR4. The idea is to move grammar analysis from generator time to parse time: at each ambiguous decision point, ALL(*) launches a subset-construction-style lookahead that explores all viable alternatives in parallel over the actual input, building a prediction DFA lazily and caching it for future visits. When regular lookahead is insufficient, it falls back to GLL-style exploration. The earlier LL(*) algorithm (PLDI 2011) did static analysis; ALL(*) is the adaptive, dynamic successor that removed LL(*)'s grammar restrictions.

sources.label: Parr, Harwell, Fisher — ALL(*) tech report (OOPSLA 2014)

sections.title: ALL(*) and adaptive LL parsing
sections.content:
The worst case is theoretically O(n⁴), but Parr and Harwell report that ALL(*) consistently runs linearly on real grammars and outperforms GLR/GLL by orders of magnitude on practical inputs. The algorithm handles any non-left-recursive CFG directly; direct left recursion is rewritten by ANTLR4's front-end into the equivalent Pratt-like precedence-climbing form.

Adaptive lookahead caching means cold paths pay a two-pass analyze-then-parse cost; warm paths are linear because the prediction DFA is reused.

sections.title: Grammar authoring and generated parsers
sections.content:
ALL(*) is why ANTLR4 displaced yacc/bison for many grammar-first users: grammars stay readable (no manual precedence disambiguation or conflict refactoring), lookahead is effectively unlimited, and the generated parsers produce decent error messages out of the box.

ANTLR4 generates parsers in Java, C#, Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, C++, Swift, PHP, and Dart. Grammar files (`.g4`) can be reused across these targets, which makes ANTLR4 attractive when one grammar must drive parsers in multiple host languages.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content: The practical limits are code size, the two-pass analyze-then-parse overhead on cold code paths, and the same "generated parser is a black box" pressures that push production compilers towards hand-written recursive descent. Editor-grade incremental reparsing and contextual error recovery are harder to retrofit into a generated ANTLR4 parser than to maintain in a hand-written one — which is why tools like Ruff migrated away from a generated parser as they matured.

sources.label: Parr, Harwell, Fisher — ALL(*) tech report (OOPSLA 2014)
